In this lesson, we're going to be talking about assessing and understanding marketplace sophistication. And this is one of the most valuable things that you can learn as a marketer. You get this lesson and it will forever change the way you craft marketing messages. In fact, after this lesson, you'll understand why lots of marketing messages, lots of marketing funnels fall flat. It's that valuable. So what is marketplace sophistication? Well, marketplace sophistication was popularized years ago by legendary advertising copywriter Eugene Schwartz. And marketplace sophistication is really all about the idea that markets prospects become more and more quote unquote sophisticated over time as they're exposed to more and more marketing messages. So as competitors come into a market, come into a niche and begin knocking each other off and making similar promises and claims, the market becomes more and more sophisticated and those type of propositions begin working less and less. And you'll see that there are five different stages of marketplace sophistication. And it's critical that you understand the sophistication level of your market of your prospects so that you can craft a message that is appropriate for their sophistication level. Now let me give you an example of what marketplace sophistication looks like. Let's take for example, the fat burning supplement arena. Well, at one point in time, there was no fat burning supplement. There were no fat burners available on the market whatsoever. And then somebody came along, some company came along and they released the first fat burner. Now, in this stage when there are no competitors, when it's a brand new market, so to speak, your marketing message doesn't have to be anything more than in this example, take this pill and lose weight. But what happened in that market, like what happens in every other market is that over time, more and more competitors came into this arena and began offering their own fat burning supplements, making similar claims. And so when that happened, the market, the prospects for fat burning supplements became more sophisticated and this general take this pill and lose weight appeal began working less and less. Now, let me step back for one second and say this. Imagine if you and I partnered together today to create a new fat burner and offer a new fat burner to the marketplace and we rolled out with a marketing campaign that had as a headline, take this pill and lose weight. What would happen? Well, we would get crushed, right? We would get crushed because that type of appeal no longer works in the fat burning arena because of the fact that prospects are too sophisticated for that because they've seen more and more advertisements. And so getting back to our example, what happened was the market became more sophisticated and then marketers became more sophisticated. Their marketing message, I should really say, went up to stage two. And stage two of marketplace sophistication is really all about expanding on the claim, expanding on the promise. And so in this case, it became take this pill and lose 15 pounds. Now, like I said, what happens in all arenas, in all markets happened here and competitors began knocking each other off, making bigger and bigger claims and the market became more and more sophisticated. You reach a point where with marketplace sophistication where you can no longer just blow up the claim, you can no longer just expand the claim and make it bigger because it loses credibility. For example, if we said take this pill and lose 100 pounds, the market today, of course, wouldn't respond to that because they wouldn't believe it because it lacks credibility. So when you reach a stage where you can no longer blow up the claim, that is the third stage of marketplace sophistication. And marketplace sophistication at stage three is all about telling how the actual product works, how it does what it is that you are claiming it does. And so in this case, it would be take this pill with Garcini Cambodia to prevent the absorption of fat so you lose weight rapidly. The focus at this stage, when a market has reached a stage two or beyond stage two where you can no longer blow up the claim, now it is about giving the mechanism, if you will, behind how the actual product delivers the results. Then of course, as more and more companies came out in this example with Garcini Cambodia, now the marketplace reaches another level. You can't today just come out with a product that says take this pill with Garcini and you'll lose weight because the market now is too sophisticated for that. At one point before the first supplement contained Garcini Cambodia, when the first product hit with Garcini, that was good enough. But now the market is too sophisticated for that. And so we reach stage four where stage four, very similar to stage two is about differentiating the mechanism, giving a unique mechanism and expanding on the claim. And so in this example, it's take this pill with pharmaceutical grade Garcini Cambodia and prevent the absorption of fat and lose up to 20 pounds in 20 days, for example. So we've got this unique mechanism now, the unique aspect in this case being let's assume that we are the first supplement to have pharmaceutical grade Garcini in it and we've expanded on the claim. Now when a market has reached the next stage of sophistication, which is the highest stage of sophistication, it no longer becomes about the product or the claim of the product or even the unique mechanism. At stage five, it becomes about identifying with the prospect and their experience. And so in this case, it's tired of taking fat burning supplements and not losing weight. Now the reality is that most markets and this is, you know, every market is different, every niche is different and you need to assess your marketplace sophistication so that you understand where they're at. But most markets fall around stage three, stage four, where you're able to use a unique mechanism, which we're going to talk about in just a few minutes to differentiate your claim. The thing that I want you to really understand that I want you to get when you get this, if you get this, is that if you don't understand your marketplace sophistication, if you don't understand what level of sophistication level your marketplace is at, if you don't take the time to look at your competitors and what it is that they've said and are saying to the market, well, you have no way of knowing if your message is up to the sophistication level. If again, we came out with a message today that was take this pill and lose 15 pounds, it's not sophisticated enough for the marketplace. And if you go and you look at a substantial amount of marketing online, a substantial amount of marketing funnels and how they're being presented, you'll see why they are falling flat, why they're not successful because they have a message that is not sophisticated enough for the marketplace. Now, I want to talk a little bit about the unique mechanism and uncovering what the unique mechanism is. Now, let me quickly tell you that the unique mechanism is the unique aspect of your product or service that delivers the end result. It's the unique component, the unique piece, the unique feature, the unique aspect of your product or service that actually delivers the result to prospects that you are promising. So if we came out with the first supplement, the first fat burning supplement that had Garsenia in it, the addition of Garsenia would be the unique mechanism. Now, the unique mechanism, you can see it at play in tons of infomercials. Anytime you hear in an infomercial where they say the secret is in X, that X is the unique mechanism. So for example, with the, let's say when they are selling knives, for example, in an infomercial and they say the secret is in the German steel or the secret, the reason why this knife doesn't dull or that it can cut through a can and cut through cement, whatever, is in the German steel. That is the unique mechanism. That is the unique component of that product that delivers the end result. And it's the addition of the unique mechanism that not only differentiates your marketing message, but it gives it credibility, gives it believability. It gives prospects hope that yes, maybe this is true. Maybe this will finally work for me. The unique mechanism. And so to find your unique mechanism, ask yourself, what is the unique component of your product or service that is actually delivering the end result? Now, in some cases, you will not have a unique component. Maybe there is just nothing unique about your product. Now, if that's the case, then we have to have a totally different conversation. But oftentimes you will find that there is nothing unique about the product. Maybe for example, you have a, let's say a pizzeria and you're offering pizza to the marketplace and there are six other pizzerias within a 10 mile radius. Well, the other method for uncovering your unique mechanism is to ask yourself, what common component of your product or service is not being talked about by competitors in their marketing that you could then feature and therefore it becomes your unique mechanism. So let me give you an example from many, many years ago from an advertising legend named Claude Hopkins. Now, the story goes that Claude Hopkins was called in to work with Schlitz beer and Schlitz beer at the time had very little, if any, market share in the beer universe and they brought in Claude Hopkins to help. And so they took Claude Hopkins on a tour of their factory. And while he was being taken around the factory, he saw something going on in terms of cleaning and filtering and sterilizing. And he said to the guy showing him around, he said, hey, well, what's going on over there? And they said, oh, that's our sterilization process. Every bottle or whatever it is, all of our beer is sterilized for purity. And Claude Hopkins said, well, why aren't you sharing that with the marketplace? Why aren't you talking about the purity, the cleaning process in your marketing? And the guy said, well, because every beer manufacturer does this. And Claude Hopkins said, but nobody is talking about it. And so they, Claude, crafted a marketing campaign built around this idea of purity and Schlitz beer leaped up to, leapt up to the top of the, is it leapt, leaped up to the top of the pack in terms of market share very quickly, simply because they identified a common element, a common component of their product that nobody else was talking about. And they use that as their unique mechanism. In this case, it's the unique mechanism because it's why is Schlitz beer better because of the purity, because of what we do, because of the sterilization process. And so that became their unique mechanism to deliver a better quality beer to the marketplace. Unfortunately, later on, Schlitz beer moved away from that positioning and moved away from having a unique mechanism and from away, away from what Claude Hopkins did. And of course, today they've got very little market share, if any, but that is another way to craft your or uncover your unique mechanism. So a couple of questions to help stimulate ideas for your, for your unique mechanism. Number one, what are the common promises competitors are making to the marketplace? What are the common product claims competitors are presenting to the marketplace? Product claims meaning claims about their product or service. And what are your top competitors using, if any, as their unique mechanism? See these three questions will help give you an idea of what it is that you can present as it relates to your product or service as the unique mechanism in your marketing. And so there you have it. That is assessing marketplace sophistication.
